lesson_title: Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.front: What is a watch window in a debugger?
cards.back: A window that displays the values of variables or expressions while the program is paused, updating as you step through code.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.text: Watch windows typically show variable {{c1::names}}, {{c2::values}}, and {{c3::types}}.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.front: What is the difference between the Locals and Watch windows?
cards.back:
Locals: Automatically shows all local variables in current scope
Watch: Shows only variables/expressions you explicitly add
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.text: You can add {{c1::expressions}} (not just variables) to a watch window, such as {{c2::x + y}} or {{c3::arr[i]}}.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.front: What happens to a variable in the watch window when it goes out of scope?
cards.back: It typically shows as unavailable or displays an error message until it comes back into scope.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.explanation:
Watch windows are useful for:
  • Tracking how key variables change as you step
  • Monitoring complex expressions
  • Comparing multiple related values
  • Spotting when a value becomes incorrect
  • Avoiding cluttering code with print statements
examples: // Watch: x, y, x+y to see calculation
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.front: Can you modify variable values while debugging?
cards.back: Yes, most debuggers allow you to change variable values while paused. This is useful for testing different scenarios without recompiling.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.text: The {{c1::Locals}} window automatically displays all variables in the {{c2::current scope}}, while {{c3::Watch}} requires manual addition.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.front: Why might you use Watch instead of just relying on the Locals window?
cards.back: Watch lets you monitor specific expressions, global variables, or values from outer scopes that aren't in the current local scope.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.text: The {{c1::call stack}} window shows the sequence of {{c2::function calls}} that led to the current point of execution.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.explanation:
Key debugger windows:
  • Locals: Current function's variables
  • Watch: User-selected variables/expressions
  • Call Stack: Function call hierarchy
  • Breakpoints: List of all breakpoints
  • Output/Console: Program output
examples: // Locals: See x, y in current function
// Call Stack: See main() → process() → calculate()
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.text: Watch windows display variable {{c1::names}}, current {{c2::values}}, and data {{c3::types}}.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables

cards.text: You can watch {{c1::expressions}} like x + y or arr[i], not just simple {{c2::variable}} names.
cards.source: 3.8 - Using an integrated debugger: Watching variables
